mnemonics.title: Kuratowski's Forbidden Graphs
mnemonics.content: K5 = Complete graph with 5 (F-I-V-E) vertices - FIVE vertices, non-planar! K3,3 = 3 and 3 partitions - Think of 3 students from class 3 interacting with 3 from class 3 (3,3) - too crowded to be planar!

mnemonics.title: Planarity Check Order
mnemonics.content: KEEP checking: K5 Exists? No - then check K3,3. If both absent, graph is Planar. Remember: KK = Kuratowski's Kriteria

acronyms.usage: Fundamental non-planar graph - 5 vertices all connected to each other, like a 5-way conference where everyone meets everyone

acronyms.expansion: Complete bipartite graph with 3 vertices in each partition
acronyms.usage: Two groups of 3 people, everyone in group A meets everyone in group B - like boys-girls dating scenario
When checking planarity: K5? K3,3? Both absent? Then Planar!

visualTips.visualization: Draw as pentagon with all diagonals - notice at least 5 crossings are unavoidable. Imagine 5 points around a circle, connect every pair - creates a 'star' inside
Draw two rows of 3 dots each. Connect every dot in top row to every dot in bottom row. You'll see it naturally forms a 'ladder' that must cross in the middle
Think of adding 'rest stops' along highways. An edge from A to B becomes A to Stop to B - like adding intermediate stations on a train route

analogies.analogy: Think of 5 people at a round table where everyone wants to shake hands with everyone else. Some hands must cross over each other to reach across the table!
Imagine 3 boys and 3 girls at a dance. If every boy wants to dance with every girl, the dance floor becomes too crowded with pairs crossing each other!
It's like a 'metal detector' for planarity - just check for these two 'forbidden coins' (K5 and K3,3) and you know if your graph is 'safe' (planar) or not
